title_en: Mu'an'an, Mubham, 'Ali, Nazil, and the Categories of Chain Characteristics
title_ar: المعنعن والمبهم والعالي والنازل وسائر الأنواع
content_en:
Al-Bayquniyyah continues its classification of hadith types with a series of categories that relate to specific features of the chain of transmission. These include the Mu'an'an, the Mubham, the 'Ali, and the Nazil — each identifying a particular characteristic of how the chain is structured or what information it contains.

The Mu'an'an hadith takes its name from the Arabic word 'an (عن), meaning 'from' or 'on the authority of.' A mu'an'an chain is one in which a transmitter uses the formula 'on the authority of X' (عن فلان) rather than explicitly stating that he heard or received the report directly. The use of 'an does not in itself indicate a break in the chain, but it raises the question of whether the transmitter actually heard the report directly from his source or merely transmitted it through an intermediary without saying so. Hadith scholars developed the concept of 'an'ana of a mudallis (one who conceals defects in chains) as particularly problematic: a known mudallis who uses 'an rather than 'heard' (سمعت) may be concealing a weak intermediary. The resolution is that 'an'ana from a non-mudallis transmitter who is established to have met his source is treated as equivalent to explicit hearing.

The Mubham (ambiguous/unnamed) hadith is one in which a transmitter in the chain is not identified by name but is referred to ambiguously — as 'a man' (rajul), 'someone' (fulan), or 'a shaykh' (shaykh). The anonymity of the transmitter makes it impossible to evaluate his reliability, which means the hadith cannot be fully authenticated. However, if other chains of the same hadith identify the anonymous transmitter, his identity can be established retroactively. The study of mubham narrators — identifying unnamed individuals in hadith chains — was itself a specialized subdiscipline, and works were composed specifically listing and identifying these figures.

The 'Ali (elevated or high) and Nazil (lowered or descending) categories relate to the length of the chain. An 'ali (elevated) hadith is one transmitted through fewer intermediate links between the compiler and the Prophet. A nazil (descending) hadith is one that requires more intermediate links. In general, shorter chains are preferred because each additional link introduces an additional possibility of error or unreliability. When a later scholar can cite a hadith through only three or four transmitters while his contemporaries need six or seven to reach the same report, the shorter chain is considered a mark of scholarly excellence and is particularly sought after. The famous rihlah (scholarly journey) tradition in Islam — the practice of traveling long distances to obtain hadiths from their closest living sources — was largely motivated by the desire to obtain 'ali (elevated) isnad.

Al-Bayquniyyah also addresses the Muttasil al-Isnad (connected chain), the Mu'allaq (suspended), and the Mursal Khafi (concealed mursal). The Mu'allaq hadith is one in which one or more links at the beginning of the chain (closest to the compiler) have been omitted. The major hadith collections, including Sahih al-Bukhari, contain some mu'allaq reports that are not presented with full isnads in the main text but may be found with complete chains elsewhere in the collection or in other sources. Al-Bukhari's use of mu'allaq reports is a topic of extensive discussion in the commentaries on his Sahih, particularly in Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani's monumental Fath al-Bari.

Taken together, the full taxonomy of hadith types that al-Bayquniyyah presents — running to thirty-four categories in the poem itself and expanded greatly in the commentaries — represents the cumulative achievement of centuries of Muslim scholarly reflection on how to evaluate transmitted knowledge. The poem serves as a mnemonic gateway into this vast and technically demanding field, providing students with the conceptual framework they need before advancing to the study of the major hadith collections and their commentaries. The enduring popularity of al-Bayquniyyah in Islamic education reflects both its pedagogical excellence and the continued centrality of hadith sciences in Muslim scholarly life.
content_ar: المعنعن ما رُوي بلفظ عن، والمبهم ما فيه راوٍ لم يُسمَّ، والعالي ما قلّ عدد رجاله بالنسبة لإسناد آخر لنفس الحديث.
